Okay, so now that we got kind of that bridge from yesterday to today, we're going to start shifting more and more into stories. So I got one other thing I want to share first before we kind of go into story structure. And this is something that at the first time we all met and talked about this kind of stuff last summer, we ended up, I thought it was going to be like a 10 minute session and I'm taking three hours. But I think we kind of figured it out. So this comes back to a concept that I call in the book and I call a lot of places called the one thing or the big domino. And so a couple of stories that kind of set this up so it makes sense. When I was talking to Perry Belcher and I were having a conversation one late night, he had a little bit to drink, which makes it way easier to get all the good stuff from people, right? And I'm the Mormon dude who doesn't drink. So I'm like, actually like, oh yeah, everyone's drunk and they share all the best stuff then. Right? The big benefit of like hanging out after hours when you don't drink. So I'm listening to all this stuff and I'm getting all these things, right? And I'm asking Perry and I'm like, so like, because he's one of the best copywriters I've ever met. And I was like, what are the things you're learning? What have you figured out? He said the biggest thing that we've learned, and this is Perry, his company, but also like digital marketers and everything, is just, he said the biggest thing we found, all the offers we've ever created, and any time our message tried to convince them of more than one thing, they bombed. He said if we try to convince them of two things or three things, like they always didn't work. He said the ones that were all the blockbuster wins, the only thing we were trying to do is get them to believe one thing through the sales message. If we had, the one consistency was that, it's one thing. And I started looking and I was looking at a lot of my things I was trying to sell at the time, like I'm trying to convince them of this, oh, and this, and then I'm trying to convince them of all these different things. And as soon as you do that, like it waters it down and it just, it stops sales instantly. And like when I heard that, I was like, God, that's really, really cool. And then a couple months later, there was another event and Tim Ferriss was on stage talking and he's doing this whole thing. And you're gonna hear him speak in person. He's a great writer, like he's so boring on like as a speaker, like I was just like, ah, and I was kind of like, anyway, it was kind of painful to sit through to be completely honest. And the end of it, like Q and A and I was like listening and some lady came up and she's like, I have a question for you, Tim, like what does your normal day look like? And I was like, that's actually really cool question. I was really excited to hear the answer to it, right? And so he's like, okay, well, he's like, I've actually had a bunch of people who have asked me if they could like come and film a day in the life of Tim Ferriss and see what I do. He's like, like he told him no because if you saw what I actually did every day, it's like insanely boring. He's like, I wake up in the morning, I drink some coffee, then like I'll read a book and I think for a while and then I meditate and then I go get my blood drawn and then I go back and I think some more and I meditate and then he's like, I'll do that for three or four or five weeks at a time before I do anything. And he's like, what most people do is they wake up in the morning, they got to do this and try to do a thousand different things and they're going through everything and try and knock down all these different to-dos and get them all done. He's like, my philosophy is I want to sit there and I want to think for a long time and figure out what's the one big domino that if I knock that domino down, it knocks down all the other dominoes and makes them irrelevant. And there's only lessons from like a personal development like destruction, but I was thinking about it from a sales standpoint how similar it is as well. Like I can go try to convince them a thousand different things, but what if there was one thing, if I can convince you of this one thing that it is the big domino that it automatically knocks everything else down and makes any other concerns or objections or anything else you have irrelevant or it makes them just disappear. I'm like, what is that one thing? So every sales message that we're creating, I'm like, what's the one thing? If I can figure out that one thing, then what's the one big domino that if we figure that out, everything else disappears. And so that's kind of, and when you come, we go deep in the perfect webinar, you'll see that it's all based on this. Like the perfect webinar is not convincing you of like one thing and three different secrets. Like three secrets are all, like everything is to knock down the one thing, like the one big domino. And so like that's what we got to figure out in every single thing. So I started going through this. I'm like, well how do we, because in theory that's, it's easy, right? Okay, cool. I need one thing. So if we figure that out, it's a little more difficult. And so last summer when we were talking about this, you guys remember when we were on the whiteboard and we were going through like 20 different variations trying to figure it out. And finally we got things to fit into this sentence. And this is the big domino sentence. So it says, if I can make them believe that my new opportunity is the key to what they desire most and it's only attainable through my specific vehicle, then all of the objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. Okay, so that's the structure of the framework. When I make a new offer, a new product, whatever it is, I'm trying to fit it into that. And if I can fit it into that, then I'm like, this is going to be homerun. I have no doubt like this is going to be perfect. Okay? So a couple examples to kind of show how this works. So in my company, like if I can make them believe that funnels is key to their online business success and is only attainable through click funnels, then all of the objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. If I can honestly convince you that the only way you can be successful is with a funnel and a click funnel is the only way you can get a funnel, you can be like, I got this expensive. I'm like, well, do you want to be successful? You have to have it. Oh, well, it's technical. Well, it's the only way to do it. You have to, like, you have to say yes. There's no other alternative. Because if I can get you to believe that, that you're either lying to yourself or you have to give me money. Like that's the only outcome. There's no other way around it, right? So that's what we have to create. Okay? Now, when we started doing this last summer, everyone's like, cool, I got it and they sent me back a thing. So I'm going to show you like what I got back from about half of you guys. It was something like, something like this. If I can make them believe that cutting calories and exercising is the key to losing weight and is only attainable through my new weight loss course, then all objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. Like, sweet Russell, I'm done. What's the next thing? And I'm like, that's a false statement, right? Like if you think about like logic, have you ever taken a logic class before? I took one and I had the coolest teacher. Otherwise it would have been really painful, but he was awesome. We did all these logical statements. And I was looking at it, I was like, that is not a true thing, right? That's like almost everyone came back. Cool, got it. What's the next thing? I'm like, no, like you missed the thing. Like this is the key. This is not true. That does knock down a big domino. Why? Because if I commit some of the cutting weight and exercising is the key, like they don't have to buy my course. They can buy 18,000 other ones. All the other ones in the red ocean I'm hanging out in. Like you can buy all of these. It's come back to that. It has to be a blue ocean and then the only way to get what you want is through in this ocean. Like all other stuff doesn't actually work, right? And that's what we have to create. So it takes a little while to think through it, but if you get it right, like it's the key of the whole thing. So if you do this and you read it, and if your statement's not true, then a couple things it means. First off, and this goes back to stuff I talked about yesterday. First off means you're not in the prolific zone. Second thing, you are not offering a new opportunity and you're not in a blue ocean. So if you struggle trying to get that sentence to work with what you do, make an actual true logical statement that's true, it means you messed up something yesterday. It means it's not actually true. Okay, so when I started working with the company, proven and did some of their sales videos and stuff, I did this exercise for myself. I'm like, okay, like what's the big domino for these guys? So I took that and this is the statement I made for myself as I went into there. If I can get them to believe that getting their body into a state of ketosis is the only way to actually lose weight, is only attainable through drink and prove its ketones, then all their objections become irrelevant. They have to give me money. If I can get you like whatever red ocean you're now trying to lose weight doesn't work. The only thing that works is actually ketosis. Put them in this blue ocean. Cool. And then there's two ways you get into ketosis. One is you have to kill yourself and it's horrible. Or number two is drink this magic drink, taste like candy and you're in ketosis. Boom, bam, no knocks over. They don't care, there's five bucks a packet. No one cares because they believe that this is the only way it happens. You guys getting that? So that's the key. This is how we have to structure our things. If you get it this way, it's putting you into a blue ocean and they have to give you money. There's no other alternative. And so, yes, Natasha. So what if you have like a proprietary way of doing something? Like you have like created a formula, a very specific system or something like that. Is that sufficient to make it work or not really? It depends. Maybe. So I'm thinking on spots, I'm not sure. So like I'm thinking about Justin and Tara's business. So they're in house flipping. And house flipping houses is not a cool thing, but their system is proprietary. So if I was doing theirs, if I didn't believe that having success in real estate is the key to their financial freedom is only, see it's hard, it's not true because then it's only attainable through our website flipping course. So what's attainable through theirs? theirs is different because they can, it's basically outsourced the whole thing. So something like if I can get them to believe that making money in real estate in less than five hours a week is the key to actually long-term success in a business, because that's tied now to the system maybe, then it's only available through our proprietary system because we're the only ones that teach the outsourcing piece, I don't know something like that. I mean, you could do it, but it's going to take a little massaging to the first part to make the second part actually true. You know what I mean? So try it out a little bit and then we'll look at it. Should we do that? Okay. Any other questions about this at all? This one seems really easy, but it's really, it takes me a while every single time to be like, okay, how do we massage this to get it? But as soon as you get that, then everything else we do becomes super easy because all I do is I can believe that. Okay, if I can believe that, that's it. A lot of times the products are commodities, right? Try not to selling commodity, which makes this statement really, really hard. It's like, okay, well, I got a bunch. If you can take a step back and like come back to the thing where it's not a commodity, like almost like James is kind of selling Trello, but Trello is free. So it's like he's got to, he's like, if I convince him that Trello is the only way they can do this thing, it's like, okay, cool, I'll go get Trello, it's free. It's like, okay, well, see that step back to the expert phase, because now it's like, now you're uncommoditizing it. I think the hardest thing you do is you're keep, you're trying to make a big domino from a commodity, which is hard. So if you can uncommoditize it by coming a layer back to the expert level, then it makes this, you know, makes the process a lot easier. What about like a bundle? Remember how it came to a thing where he had the car wash and he did three of the same car washes together and then it's like a triple step system. It's the same thing over and over. It's just like, it's commodity and it's like, how do you make this different? And I'm like, well, you can buy it five other places, so it's hard to make that different unless you come a layer back and it's like, look, we're going to defend your home. We're going to, you know, like kind of a bigger thing. And there's like the expert, like the expert layers, what makes this easy? You know what I mean? It makes it easier. That's what I'm trying to teach people. So the second one is too big, I think. Every time it's like, I can achieve anything in life. It's similar struggle I have with Joseph all the time with the hypnosis, he can hypnotize people to everything and because of that it's hard to like get people to give him money. So it's like, figuring out specific, like, like Max Wilmals was all about like plastic surgery, like you come in trying to fix your body and then you leave and you're still depressed because it wasn't the physical change, it was the mental change you have to make, right? So instead of going super broad there, like how do we shrink it down to like who you actually want to serve? So the key to helping you to, let's say it's mothers, like helping you to feel confident after babies or whatever the specific market you want to kind of go after. But just making that part more specific I think is going to help. Because the problem we all have is we go like so big, like we can help everybody and it's like yes, in theory, but the problem is then like we can't, it's too expensive to target everybody where if we can shrink it down to it's like this is who we serve and you can do multiple ones if you want to serve a lot of people but like being very clear on that, like this is who our people are, this is who we're serving, then it becomes really easy and then people hear that and it resonates with them. Like if I had like a course that's like funnels for chiropractors, which I have a friend now who does, like every chiropractor buys it because it's like, oh, like this is the only do this funnels for chiropractors, whereas anybody's would actually work but it's very specific to them. If I can make people believe that learning to switch your mental set point to like a thermostat is the key to be content with who you are and is only a tool to the feedback control mastery. It's getting better. Still being content who you are still like super, super broad. Think about like, think about the specific, like imagine you're sitting across in your office with that person that you're helping, like who is that person, like what's, how do you identify that actual person? I want to make it very tangible, like what's their problem? We don't have to answer right now, but thinking through that, like figuring out who that actual person is. Question for you Russell. So like what if there's like a verbal barrier or like a technicality barrier in what you're trying to sell? So like for example, I can make people believe that stopping crunches and setups is the key to getting a flat stomach after babies. It's only attainable through my 30 day program. So that's cool and that sounds great, but then people are like, well, how? Like what do you mean by that? And so in our program, like when you have a baby or stomach expands as it comes back together, there's a connective tissue that gets weakened. So a lot of times women will have like a lower belly, lower mommy pooch, whatever you want to call it. And, and if you have a certain amount of intraabdominal pressure, and so if you can, if you're doing crunches and setups, it's going to increase that pressure and it's never going to allow that connective tissue to heal. So this is like true in like 25% of women. So that's a lot of verbal technical jargon. I think a lot of us probably have like science behind what we're teaching or like, what's the key to keeping it really, really simple in this phrase? So that way, like everybody can understand it because to say like, oh, cool. I don't have to do crunches or sit ups and I can get a flat stomach. That sounds amazing. I'm thinking through this because it's like, that's the key. If I give them to believe that everything else domino falls down and just all everything else goes away and it becomes super, super easy.
